bull_thesis: Signed, definitive merger (March 10, 2026): Cintas acquires UniFirst for $155.00 cash + 0.7720 CTAS shares per UNF share. The two historically fatal risks to any UNF deal are already cleared: (1) the Class-B founder-family overhang is GONE — shareholders approved June 12, 2026 with >99% of votes cast in favor (~95% of all shares); (2) financing is not a concern — Cintas is investment-grade, funding via a $2.85B bridge + cash/credit to only ~1.5x pro-forma leverage, with a ~$375M synergy case. The ONLY open gate is FTC antitrust. At CTAS ~$170.51 the live deal value is ~$286.63/share ($155 cash + $131.63 stock), vs UNF ~$263 — a fat ~8.5% gross spread blown out purely by the June-11 FTC Second Request. The deal has a long contractual runway (outside date Jan 10, 2027 + two 4-month auto-extensions), so it cannot lapse on a near-term clock. Cintas accepted a $350M reverse termination fee (>6.5% of value, ~$18.70/sh), signaling it underwrote the antitrust risk and is committed to clearing it — most plausibly via divestiture of overlapping local route/plant markets. Base rate: a remedy-cleared close at ~$287 within H2-2026/H1-2027.
bear_thesis: This is a horizontal #1 (Cintas) + #2 (UniFirst) combination in US uniform rental — the textbook hard antitrust case, and the FTC issued a Second Request on June 11, 2026, exactly as feared. If the FTC sues to block rather than accept divestitures, the deal breaks. Substantial-compliance and litigation timelines are open-ended; the close slips from the dossier's stale Nov-30 date toward H1-2027 or fails outright. On a hard regulatory block, UNF re-rates toward standalone (~$180-210 pre-deal range; 52-wk low $147.66) plus the $18.70/sh reverse fee, i.e. a floor around $215-230 — well below today's ~$263, so the downside on a break (-$40+) exceeds the upside to the deal (+$24). The floating CTAS leg adds a second risk: CTAS is near its 52-wk low ($170 vs $226 high); further CTAS weakness compresses the realized consideration below $287 even if the deal closes.
sop_bull: Event-WIN value = the live deal consideration. $155.00 cash + 0.7720 x CTAS. At CTAS $170.51 that is $155.00 + $131.63 = $286.63/share. At CTAS recovering toward analyst targets (~$190-225), the stock leg is worth $147-174, lifting total consideration to ~$302-329 — the dossier's $291.07 target sits within this band on a modest CTAS recovery. Bull SoP carried at ~$291 (deal value with slight CTAS uplift).
sop_bear: Deal-BREAK floor = UNF standalone + reverse-termination fee. Standalone re-rate ~$200-210 (pre-deal trading range $180-210; 52-wk low $147.66) PLUS $350M reverse termination fee / ~18.7M shares = +$18.70/share, PLUS residual scarcity/strategic-target premium (UNF remains the obvious #2 asset, inviting a re-approach or PE bid). Net verified hard-block floor ~$222/share. This is more conservative than the dossier's stated $257.91 (which reads as a timing-slip floor, not a true antitrust-block floor).
sop_breakdown: Probability-weighted: ~78% close at ~$287 (live deal value) + ~22% antitrust block at ~$222 (standalone + $18.70/sh reverse fee). EV = 0.78 x 287 + 0.22 x 222 = 223.9 + 48.8 = ~$272-276. Carried base-case SoP fair value ~$276/share — comfortably above the live ~$263, the gap being the FTC-block risk premium the market is over-discounting given the $350M backstop and likely divestiture remedy path.
risk_reward: From ~$263: upside-to-deal ~+$24 (to ~$287, +9%); downside-to-hard-block-floor ~-$41 (to ~$222, -16%). Raw outcome ratio ~0.6:1, but probability-weighted (78% close / 22% block) the expected value ~$276 sits ABOVE the live price, i.e. positive carry with a sized, contractual ($18.70/sh) downside backstop. Defined-risk regulated arb, not open-ended.
catalyst_summary: Cintas/UniFirst merger ($155 cash + 0.7720 CTAS, ~$287 live value); signed 03-10-26, shareholders approved 06-12-26 (99%); ONLY open gate is FTC antitrust, now in Second Request (issued 06-11-26); outside date Jan-10-2027 (+2x 4-mo extensions); ~8.5% spread = FTC-block premium, ~$18.70/sh reverse-fee backstop.
dated_milestone: FTC Second Request issued 2026-06-11; HSR clock = 30 days after both parties substantially comply; company guides close H2 calendar 2026; outside/End Date 2027-01-10 with two automatic 4-month extensions (potential to ~Sep 2027).
forcing_function: FTC antitrust resolution — the agency either accepts a divestiture consent decree (deal clears, UNF converges to ~$287) or sues to block (deal breaks, UNF falls to ~$222 floor incl. $350M reverse fee). Substantial-compliance certification with the FTC starts the final 30-day HSR countdown; that filing is the trigger that collapses the spread.
consensus_delta: The street/arb desk is pricing a ~8.5% gross spread that implies a meaningfully elevated FTC-block probability following the June-11 Second Request. That over-discounts the structure: (1) Cintas voluntarily accepted a $350M reverse termination fee (>6.5% of value) — acquirers do not sign that unless they have underwritten a remedy path and intend to fight for clearance, typically via divestiture of overlapping local route/plant markets where uniform rental competition is genuinely local and divestible; (2) the contract has a long runway (Jan-10-2027 + two 4-month extensions), so the spread is partly just a time-value-of-money + Second-Request-duration discount, not a pure break-probability read; (3) the deal is already shareholder-approved (99%, 06-12) with the historically fatal Class-B family overhang resolved — a deal-completion risk that the market once treated as the binding constraint is now off the table. The mispricing is that the market is treating an FTC Second Request (routine for any #1+#2 horizontal) as closer to a block than to a remedy.
valley_of_death: Near-term (3-9 months) the risk is the Second Request grind itself: substantial compliance on a horizontal combination can take several months of document production, during which the spread can stay wide or widen on adverse FTC headlines, and the dossier's stale Nov-30 date will visibly slip toward H1-2027, frustrating time-boxed holders. Second, the floating CTAS leg: CTAS trades ~$170 near its 52-wk low ($161-227 range) — a further CTAS drawdown compresses the realized consideration below ~$287 even on a clean close, and marks the position lower in the interim. No UNF-specific cash-burn, maturity wall, dividend cut, or going-concern risk — the target balance sheet is clean. The pain is regulatory-headline volatility and CTAS beta, not solvency.
positioning_washout: Mechanical-selling risk is modest and largely already realized: the index-deletion / passive exit on a take-private is a known forward event, not a surprise that washes out holders now. The active base has already rotated from fundamental holders to merger-arb desks (the wide spread is itself the signature of arb ownership). A genuine washout would come only on an FTC-sues-to-block headline, which would force arbs to liquidate into the ~$222 floor in a single gap — that is the event to either avoid or buy into, depending on remedy read.
moderator_conclusion: Defined-risk regulated merger-arb, classified ARB (terms fixed, so upside capped at the floating ~$287 deal value) with a live PENDING_HARD regulatory gate. The two classic deal-killers are already cleared — shareholder/founder-family approval (06-12, 99%) and financing (IG acquirer, 1.5x pro-forma). The ENTIRE remaining thesis is the FTC Second Request (issued 06-11), a horizontal #1+#2 review whose resolution is the single binary node. I do NOT cap this like a priced compounder — value_conviction here is the margin of safety to the verified break floor (~$222, which includes a contractual $18.70/sh reverse fee), and that floor is real and sized. But I also do NOT award the AGGRESSIVE-ENTRY 5: the catalyst is ARB (capped upside) rather than a wide-open PENDING_HARD, the dossier's hard date (2026-11-30) is now STALE/SOFT-EXTENDED by the Second Request (realistic close H2-26/H1-27 against a Jan-10-2027 outside date + extensions), and the raw outcome ratio on a hard block is unfavorable (-16% vs +9%). The probability-weighted EV (~$276 at 78/22 odds) still sits above the live ~$263, giving positive carry. Verdict B (Watchlist for Capitulation): the clean entry is on an adverse-FTC-headline gap toward the floor, OR on confirmation of a divestiture-remedy path that collapses the block probability — either event resolves the one node that matters. Conviction 4: a real, well-backstopped arb with a long runway, penalized one notch for the capped (ARB) upside and the now-soft timeline rather than a fired or open-ended setup.
interrogator_dossier:
# UNF (UniFirst Corp) - Event-Driven Interrogation Dossier

Frame: NOT a business-quality/compounding thesis. UniFirst is the #2 US uniform-rental operator being acquired by #1 Cintas (CTAS). The thesis is the merger-arb spread between live UNF price and deal-resolved value, gated on a HARD regulatory step (HSR/FTC antitrust), with a defined downside FLOOR (deal-break standalone value cushioned by a large reverse-termination fee).

1. THE EVENT - REAL, DATED, BINDING. Definitive Agreement and Plan of Merger signed March 10, 2026; two-step merger; UNF goes private/delists on close. Followed a public courtship (Cintas first proposed $275 all-cash Dec-22-2025, rebuffed, then sweetened). Consideration per UNF share: $155.00 cash + 0.7720 CTAS shares (~$310 headline at announce, EV ~$5.5B; stock leg floats). SHAREHOLDER VOTE PASSED June 12, 2026, >99% of votes cast in favor (~95% of all common+Class B) - the historically fatal Class-B founder-family overhang is RESOLVED. Remaining gate = ANTITRUST.

2. CATALYST STATUS (verified web, as of 2026-06-22). FTC SECOND REQUEST ISSUED June 11, 2026 to both Cintas and UniFirst - the material development the static dossier date (2026-11-30) does not reflect. HSR mechanics: waiting period extends until 30 days after BOTH parties substantially comply with the Second Request. Cintas continues to expect close in H2 calendar 2026, subject to clearing FTC review (now a soft expectation). Outside/End Date = January 10, 2027 with automatic extension of up to two 4-month periods - deal does NOT auto-die at Nov-30. Verdict: PENDING_HARD/ARB hybrid - terms FIXED (upside capped ~$286-291) but the binding FTC gate is LIVE/dated/unresolved; the wide spread is the Second-Request fear premium.

3. SPREAD & ASYMMETRY (live). Live deal value = $155.00 + 0.7720 x CTAS($170.51) = $131.63 => ~$286.63/share today. Live UNF ~$262-265 (dossier $264.83). Gross spread ~$22-24/sh, ~8.5% - WIDE, blown out by the 06-11 Second Request.

4. THE FLOOR. Reverse termination fee: if antitrust blocks the deal, Cintas owes UniFirst $350M (>6.5% of value) = ~$18.70/share on ~18.7M shares. Standalone: pre-deal ~$180-210 (52-wk low $147.66). Hard-block floor ~$215-230 (standalone + break fee + residual scarcity); carried at ~$222 - more conservative than dossier's $257.91 (which reads as a timing-slip, not a hard-block, floor). Re-derived from ~$263: upside-to-deal +$24, downside-to-hard-block -$41; ~0.6:1 raw, but probability-weighted (~78% close/22% block) EV sits above live price.

5. RED/GREEN FLAGS. RED: horizontal #1+#2 (textbook hard antitrust; Second Request is the core risk); dossier date stale; CTAS-stock leg floats (CTAS near 52-wk low). GREEN: shareholder+family approval secured 06-12 (99%); $350M reverse fee (~$18.70/sh backstop + signals Cintas committed to a remedy); long runway (Jan-10-2027 +2x4mo); clean financing (1.5x pro-forma, ~$375M synergies); no CVR/going-concern/ATM/dilution at UNF.

6. ACQUIRER CREDIBILITY. Cintas is a disciplined IG strategic that raised its bid twice and accepted a $350M reverse fee - committed and credible, most plausibly clearing via divestiture of overlapping local markets. The one binary node: FTC accepts a remedy vs sues to block.

7. DIRECTOR VERDICT. Classification PIVOTING (public compounder -> deal-resolved cash-and-stock claim). Weigh: signed, shareholder-approved deal whose ONLY open gate is FTC antitrust (now Second Request); the ~8.5% spread is the block premium, partly backstopped by $18.70/sh reverse fee + long runway. Size as defined-risk regulated arb. Moat/secular N/A.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: none
skeptic_kill_fact: The catalyst is GENUINELY LIVE and the load-bearing facts survive primary-source verification: signed merger ($155.00 cash + 0.7720 CTAS), shareholder vote PASSED 06-12-2026 (>99% of votes cast, ~95% of shares) per UNF 8-K, and the ONLY open gate (FTC antitrust) is real and unresolved — the Second Request was issued 06-11-2026 to BOTH parties, extending the HSR period until 30 days after substantial compliance (CTAS 8-K). The $350M reverse-termination fee and Jan-10-2027 outside date (+up to 8mo extensions) are confirmed in the merger agreement. BUT the dossier's asymmetry is STALE on price: live UNF is ~$276 (current quote), not ~$263. At UNF $276 / CTAS ~$170 the deal value ~$287 leaves upside of only ~+$11 (+4%), while a hard-block floor ~$222 is -$54 (-20%) — a far WORSE risk/reward (~0.2:1 raw) than the dossier's +9%/-16% claim. The spread has already substantially collapsed; the easy carry is gone.

catalyst.dated_milestone: FTC Second Request issued 2026-06-11; HSR clock = 30 days after both parties substantially comply; company guides close H2 calendar 2026; outside/End Date 2027-01-10 with two automatic 4-month extensions (potential to ~Sep 2027).
catalyst.binding_reason: Signed Cintas/UniFirst merger, shareholder + founder-family overhang cleared (99% on 06-12), ONLY open gate is FTC antitrust (Second Request 06-11) on a textbook #1+#2 horizontal. Skeptic capped at 3 (~60) AND flagged the spread has collapsed: at the corrected live ~$276 vs ~$287 deal value, upside is only ~+4% while a hard-block floor (~$222) is ~-20% — a ~0.2:1 raw ratio with EV now sitting essentially AT the live price. Capped upside (ARB), stale-and-soft timeline (Jan-10-2027 outside date + extensions), and the easy carry is gone — below the seat line.

bull_thesis: This is a merger-arbitrage bet: we are buying the gap between UniFirst's current price (about $260) and what the Cintas takeover pays if it closes. Cintas, the largest uniform-rental and workplace-supplies company in North America, agreed on March 10, 2026 to buy UniFirst, the number-three player, for $155.00 in cash plus 0.7720 of a Cintas share per UniFirst share. At Cintas's recent price near $172, that package is worth roughly $288 today, a gross spread of about 9-11% from here. The selling side is fully committed: UniFirst shareholders approved the deal on June 12, 2026 with more than 99% of votes in favor, so there is no vote risk left. The acquirer is a deep-pocketed strategic with no financing contingency. If antitrust regulators clear the combination, the spread converges and the position earns a high-single-digit to low-double-digit return over the next six to twelve months. Even on a failure, a $350 million reverse break fee (about $18-19 per share) gives a partial cash cushion. The bull case depends on one condition above all others: the U.S. Federal Trade Commission ultimately allowing the deal, possibly after a fight or a divestiture of overlapping routes.
bear_thesis: The whole position is hostage to a contested antitrust review. On June 11, 2026 both companies received a Second Request from the Federal Trade Commission under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Act, the regulator's signal that it is scrutinizing the deal hard. This is not routine here: the merger combines the number-one and number-three competitors in a concentrated market, the textbook profile for resistance, a forced divestiture, or an outright block. If the deal breaks, UniFirst stops being worth the takeout price and reverts to its standalone value. Before any deal talk in December 2025 the stock traded around $180-190; a clean standalone fair value today, crediting its margin-improvement progress, is roughly $200-225. Even with the $18-19-per-share break fee as a cushion, a realistic break floor is about $210-225, roughly 14-20% below today's price. Compounding the risk, nearly half the consideration is Cintas stock, and Cintas has already fallen about 20% since the deal was struck, eroding the headline value and adding a second source of variability. The contractual deadline runs to January 10, 2027 with extensions to roughly September 2027, so this is a patient, uncertain hold, not an imminent payout. The wide spread is the market pricing real regulatory risk, not a free lunch.
consensus_delta: Our board's own dossier was offside, not just the street. The Catalyst-Watch entry carried a downside floor of $257.91 and a 3.79:1 risk/reward, which implied near-costless asymmetry. Live verification refutes that: a deal break sends UniFirst back to a standalone value of roughly $210-225 (it traded $180-190 before the December 2025 deal talk), so the true downside is about -18%, not -1%. The corrected risk/reward is roughly even money. Where the market is arguably right is in keeping a wide ~10% spread: it is pricing real antitrust risk on a #1-buys-#3 combination that has already drawn an FTC Second Request. The mispricing, if any, is in our own stale floor, not in the public quote.
forcing_function: FTC antitrust clearance (or block) of the Cintas/UniFirst combination. The hard trigger is expiration/termination of the extended HSR waiting period after substantial compliance with the June 11, 2026 Second Request; the contractual backstop is the January 10, 2027 outside date with extensions to roughly September 2027.
valley_of_death: The next three to nine months are a regulatory waiting game with a falling-stock-component overhang. The binding event is the FTC's response to the June 11, 2026 Second Request; substantial compliance can take many months, and the agency may demand divestitures or sue to block. Meanwhile nearly half the consideration is Cintas stock, and Cintas has already dropped about 20% since the deal was signed, so further weakness in Cintas erodes the live deal value even if the merger stays on track. There is no cash-burn or maturity wall here (both companies are healthy), but the spread can widen sharply on any adverse antitrust headline well before the catalyst resolves.
positioning_washout: Limited mechanical-selling dynamics. With the shareholder vote already passed (99%+ on June 12, 2026), the holder base has rotated substantially toward merger-arbitrage funds who are positioned for the spread, not toward yield or passive holders who would be forced to liquidate. The main forced-flow risk runs the other way: a deal break would trigger arb funds to dump simultaneously, accelerating the fall toward the standalone floor.
moderator_conclusion: UniFirst is a genuine, dated, shareholder-approved merger arbitrage, but the verified facts make it a wait-and-watch rather than an aggressive entry. The catalyst is real and binding, yet it is gated on a contested antitrust review that has already drawn an FTC Second Request on a number-one-buys-number-three combination, with the contractual deadline as far out as January 2027 (extendable to roughly September 2027). Critically, correcting the downside floor from the board's stale $257.91 to a realistic standalone value of about $215 collapses the asymmetry from a headline 3.79:1 to roughly even money: about +10% to the ~$288 deal value versus about -18% to a break. The added wrinkle that nearly half the payout floats with Cintas stock, which has already fallen ~20%, removes the clean defined-risk profile a cash deal would offer. This does not earn the aggressive-entry treatment reserved for a live hard catalyst with a verified floor well below the target. The right posture is to watch for the FTC's path to clear: meaningful spread-narrowing on a clearance signal, evidence of a workable divestiture remedy, or a wider spread that restores real asymmetry would be the trigger to size up. Until then, the antitrust tail risk and corrected break floor justify patience.
sop_breakdown: Probability-weighted base case. Deal-success value ~$288 and deal-break floor ~$215. Assigning roughly 55% odds the FTC ultimately clears (the Second Request makes this far from certain for a #1-buys-#3 combination) and 45% to a break or block: 0.55 x 288 + 0.45 x 215 = about $255. Haircutting modestly for the long, uncertain timeline (clearance not contractually due until January 2027, extendable to ~September 2027) and the stock-component variability lands a base-case fair value near $245 per share.
risk_reward: Roughly even money on corrected numbers. Upside to deal value ~$288 is about +10% from ~$262; downside to a realistic break floor ~$215 is about -18%. That is approximately 0.6:1 upside-to-downside, NOT the 3.79:1 the board's stale $257.91 floor implied. The wide spread reflects genuine antitrust risk, not free asymmetry.
interrogator_dossier:
# UniFirst Corporation (UNF) — Event Dossier

**Setup:** This is a merger arbitrage situation, not a business-quality bet. UniFirst, the third-largest uniform-rental and workplace-supplies company in North America, has agreed to be bought by Cintas, the industry's clear number one. The whole thesis lives or dies on whether antitrust regulators let the deal close. The franchise underneath is fine but unremarkable; we are buying the gap between today's price and what the deal pays, not the company.

## 1. Is the catalyst real, dated, and binding?

Yes on all three, with one important caveat about the timeline.

- **The agreement is signed and shareholder-approved.** Cintas and UniFirst signed a definitive merger agreement on March 10, 2026. UniFirst shareholders approved it on June 12, 2026, with more than 99% of votes cast in favor. The selling side of this deal is fully committed — there is no shareholder-vote risk left.
- **The terms are fixed.** Each UniFirst share converts into $155.00 in cash plus 0.7720 of a Cintas share. That mix matters: a little over half the consideration is cash (firm) and a little under half floats with Cintas's stock price (variable). The deal was first framed as roughly $310 per share back in December 2025, but because Cintas stock has fallen since then (from the low-$200s to about $170), the live value of the package has compressed.
- **The binding gate is a hard date with a clear trigger.** The merger agreement carries an outside date (the contractual deadline after which either side can walk) of January 10, 2027, with automatic extensions of up to eight months if antitrust clearance is the only thing still outstanding. So the true backstop date is potentially as late as roughly September 2027. Our board's working catalyst date of November 30, 2026 is a reasonable mid-point estimate for clearance, but it is an estimate, not a contractual milestone — the only firm dates are the January 2027 outside date and its extensions.

## 2. The single biggest risk: the FTC Second Request

This is the load-bearing fact of the entire situation. On June 11, 2026, both companies received a "Second Request" from the U.S. Federal Trade Commission under the Hart-Scott-Rodino Act (the law requiring large mergers to clear an antitrust review). A Second Request is a demand for a large volume of additional documents and data, and it is the regulator's signal that it is taking a hard look. It restarts the clock: the waiting period now runs until 30 days after both companies "substantially comply" with the demand.

Why this is serious here and not routine: this deal combines the number-one and number-three players in North American uniform rental and facility services. That is exactly the kind of horizontal combination — fewer large competitors in a concentrated market — that draws the most regulatory resistance. Some segments and local markets could approach near-monopoly concentration. A Second Request on a deal like this is not a rubber stamp; it raises a real possibility of an extended fight, a required divestiture of overlapping routes or plants, or an outright block.

## 3. The verified downside floor — and why our board's number is too generous

If the deal breaks, UniFirst stops being worth the deal price and reverts to its standalone value. Here is the honest reckoning:

- Before any deal talk (December 2025), UniFirst traded in roughly the $180–190 area. The stock has since climbed partly on the deal and partly on a genuine margin-improvement story, with a year-to-date gain of about 35%.
- A clean standalone fair value today, crediting the operational improvement, is roughly $200–225 per share. That is the gravity the stock falls back toward on a break.
- There is one real cushion: Cintas owes UniFirst a reverse termination fee of $350 million if antitrust action kills the deal — about $18-$19 per share. That partly softens a break.

Putting those together, a realistic deal-break floor is roughly **$210–225**, not the $257.91 our Catalyst-Watch board carried. The board's floor appears to anchor on a near-current price; that overstates the protection. The downside is real and larger than the dossier number suggests — the loss on a break is on the order of 14–20% from today's ~$262, not the 1-2% the stale floor implies.

## 4. The asymmetry, recomputed on live numbers

- **Live UniFirst price:** about $259–265.
- **Live deal value:** $155 cash + (0.7720 × ~$171.90 Cintas) ≈ **$288** today. (Our board's $291 target assumes a slightly higher Cintas price; both are in the same neighborhood.)
- **Gross spread to the deal:** roughly 9–11% from here if it closes.
- **Downside to a realistic break floor (~$215):** roughly 17%.

So the true risk/reward is closer to **even money (about 0.6:1 upside-to-downside)**, not the 3.79:1 the stale floor implied. The spread is wide precisely because the market is pricing meaningful antitrust risk — that width is information, not a free lunch. An additional wrinkle: because nearly half the consideration is Cintas stock, the "value" you capture also depends on where Cintas trades at close, adding a second source of variability a pure cash deal would not have.

## 5. Red flags and green flags

🔴 Second Request from the FTC on a number-one-buys-number-three combination — the central, deal-defining risk.
🔴 Stock-heavy consideration means the payoff floats with Cintas, and Cintas has fallen ~20% since the deal was struck, eroding the headline price.
🔴 The realistic break floor sits well below today's price; the downside is genuine, not nominal.
🟢 Shareholder vote cleared with 99%+ approval — no vote risk remains.
🟢 A $350 million reverse break fee gives UniFirst (and the arb) a partial cash backstop if antitrust blocks it.
🟢 The acquirer is a deep-pocketed strategic with obvious financing capacity; there is no financing contingency or going-concern question on either side.

## 6. Credibility verdict for the director

The catalyst is real, binding, and approved by shareholders — but it is gated on a contested antitrust review that has already drawn a Second Request, and the contractual deadline (January 2027, extendable to ~September 2027) is far enough out that this is a patient, uncertain hold rather than an imminent re-rate. The asymmetry is roughly symmetric once the floor is corrected to a real standalone value, which is a meaningfully worse setup than the board's stale numbers suggested. This is a live, dated merger-arb with genuine regulatory tail risk, not a clean defined-risk layup.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: none
